substrate_condition: clean, uniform dark brown clay/lava-rock style pellet substrate with no visible debris
concerns: Moss clump appears unanchored or loosely resting on substrate — fronds are splaying outward and upward in a somewhat disheveled pattern, which may indicate it has detached or is not tied down
Some stem/branch material visible at the moss base appears dark or blackened, worth monitoring for die-off
narrative: A dense, bright green moss clump — likely java moss or a similar fine-leaved aquatic moss — sits directly on the substrate near the rear corner of the tank, with one vivid red cherry shrimp visible nestled at its base, actively grazing or resting. The moss shows healthy green coloration overall but has some wispy, loose fronds extending outward and a few darker sections at the base that may warrant a closer look.
owner_comment: moss looks pretty healthy and does not appear to be melting

substrate_condition: clean, dark granular substrate — appears uniform and undisturbed
concerns: Mesh media bag sitting directly on substrate in the open — may be worth checking its contents and confirming placement is intentional for the cycle
Floating plants at the surface appear sparse/fragmented — worth monitoring coverage
narrative: A wide, low-angle shot taken from substrate level reveals the tank's interior clearly: a white mesh media bag (likely crushed coral, limestone, or similar buffering media) rests prominently in the midground, flanked by a feathery green plant on the left and a multi-stage sponge/ceramic filter unit on the right. At least one shrimp — a small red individual — is visible peeking from behind or beneath the mesh bag near center-right, identifiable by a flash of red. Water surface shows active rippling from the blue-tipped spray bar or airline, with what appears to be floating plants (possibly frogbit or duckweed) clustered at the surface.
image_subject: full tank shot — low substrate-level angle, Day 28 of nitrogen cycle

substrate_condition: dark granular substrate, appears clean with no visible debris accumulation outside the feeding area
concerns: A mesh feeding dish or breeder net is laid flat on the substrate — if this is a feeding station, the food load appears quite large and should be monitored to avoid fouling the water during an active nitrogen cycle
Water column shows a faint whitish haze against the back glass — could be early bacterial bloom consistent with day 28 of cycling, worth monitoring
narrative: A mesh feeding cloth or fine-net feeding dish dominates the foreground, laden with what appears to be a substantial amount of food — two small red shrimp are visible actively foraging on or near it. In the background, the sponge filter (with visible bio-media balls in the lower chamber) and a heater with flow head are both submerged and appear operational, with green floating plants visible at the waterline.
image_subject: tank interior — feeding station, equipment, and shrimp foraging
